At 2:00, she describes the punishment of a friend's mother for a ""crime"" that in the U.S. is just a normal Friday night activity. At 3:00, when she talks about what happened to her mom, I almost lose it. But at 4:40, she talks about some ways to help that I think we should all hear.

headline: They're Called The Silent Victims. It's Time We Raised Our Voices To Help.
excerpt: If Ray Rice’s video made us care about domestic violence, then this audio should make us care about the kids
lede: One of the most interesting things about NFL star Ray Rice’s domestic violence incident was how much more attention it got after the video of him striking his fiancé in the elevator was finally made public. As if we needed a vivid imagination to figure out that something terrible happened when two people entered an elevator, and only one, the one who runs into 300 pound linemen on a weekly basis, exited upright. But the reality is, it was one thing to read about it, but something else entirely to see it. When you can actually empathize with the humanity of the situation, your perspective changes.
share_text: A six year old girl helps put a voice to the silent victims of domestic violence. This is a heavy but important one.

Don't be that parent.

headline: How It Feels To Make The Parents Who Love You More Uncomfortable Than They've Ever Been Before
lede:
In my dating life, I have a few moments that stand out to me. When I was 20, I literally kissed a guy in the summer when it started to rain on our first date. (I mean, you couldn't ask for a more Romantic Comedy starring YOU kind of moment, am I right?) When I was a junior in art college, I had my first serious boyfriend. So serious, that we met each other's parents over Christmas break. I met his in Brooklyn, and then the next week, over Christmas, he met mine in Washington DC.

The following poem I wrote after the experience of having him stay over my parents house. They're Haitian, very religious and quite homophobic, still — unfortunately. They love me, but the ""being-gay-is-bad"" mantra was driven into them when they were young and now they're elderly (both in their 70s).

I'm still pulling for 'em.

From a guy who's been on the receiving end of years of low-grade hate from family members (not all of them!), maybe think about how it feels to be on the other side of your discomfort with someone's life. And ask yourself why you feel so uncomfortable, maybe.

headline: It Took 25,782,500 Kids To Begin To Undo The Damage Caused By 1 Doctor
excerpt: Here's the deadly results when people say things about science without actually looking at science.
lede:
Once upon a time, a scientist named Dr. Andrew Wakefield published in the medical journal The Lancet that he had discovered a link between autism and vaccines. After years of controversy and making parents mistrust vaccines, along with collecting $674,000 from lawyers who would benefit from suing vaccine makers, it was discovered he had made the whole thing up. The Lancet publicly apologized and reported that further investigation led to the discovery that he had fabricated everything.

In the intervening years, millions have been spent on studying this further to see if there was anything that could connect autism and vaccines. This is what they found.

headline: The Truth About The Ferguson Case That Most People Won't Acknowledge
excerpt: Some things we've learned about the Ferguson case that you might not know.
lede:
Some things I have learned:

Pro Publica recently did an in-depth analysis and found that black kids between the ages of 15-19 are 21 times more likely to be shot and killed by cops than white kids of the same age. That's an insane statistic. That's per capita, not actual numbers. If you are a black teenage boy, you have a 21x higher risk of being shot by police.

Also, in 2010, Federal prosecutors took 162,000 cases to a grand jury. You know how many DIDN'T go to trial? 6.

Also, Mike Brown's family will never get a state criminal trial to get justice for their son. And the testimony that denied them that right technically doesn't make any sense. Seriously, go read it.

headline: He's Going To Argue With Himself For 13 Minutes. And Then You'll Walk Away A Lot Smarter. I Mean It.
lede:
The Internet has been wonderful for a lot of things. Connecting people across geographic boundaries? Check. Containing the widest and deepest store of information ever before assembled in the history of humanity? Check. Binging on Game Of Thrones? Check. Clear-headed and productive arguments? Not so much.

Well, the best way to change that is to start naming and shaming the arguments that go off the rails. The following isn’t a comprehensive list of the types of logical fallacies you’re likely to encounter on the Internet, but it’s a pretty good start.

headline: Don't Believe In Two Different Americas? Here's All The Proof You Need. Which One Do You Live In?
lede: When we talk about police brutality and racial profiling, there's inevitably people who don't understand the issue because of their own positive interactions with police officers. Now, are all cops bad and violent? No, of course not. But sadly, there are too many instances where police are unnecessarily hostile and aggressive when dealing with law abiding black citizens. Two Twitter hashtags, #CrimingWhileWhite and #AliveWhileBlack perfectly illustrate the very different world's white and black Americans are living in.

headline: Here’s What Can Happen When A Country Really Makes Education A National Priority
lede:
The Overseas Development Institute (ODI) has just released a new report on Mongolia that's amazing. Even though up to 40% of its population is nomadic, the country has found a way to achieve something wealthier nations can only envy: 98% of its girls and 93% of its boys are now getting a secondary education.

Mongolians value the education of their kids almost above everything else, and now their government — flush with money from a mining boom and improved taxation — has taken up the challenge of paying for it. With a completely overhauled modern education policy that's more responsive to families' needs, and with the support of external financing partners, they're actually getting it done.

headline: May This Be The Very Last People-Dying-From-Guns Interpretive Dance You Ever See
lede:
I love an interpretive dance, guys. The emotion! The things to interpret without words! The things to feeeel. This dance in particular features teenagers (TEENAGERS?!) and a funky band (FUNKY BAND!) that includes a tuba (TUBA!) and a melodica (MELODICA!) all dancing out their rage. I know! It’s a lot of random things and it adds up to some unexpected intensity all set to a catchy tune.

I wouldn’t usually say “yes hand the keys to the teenagers!” but in this case? HAND THE KEYS TO THE TEENAGERS!

headline: An Empowering Look At The Ways Media Discriminates Against A Certain Body Type And How Wrong That Is
lede:
Fat is not lazy (skinny people can be lazy too). Fat is not unhealthy (skinny people can be unhealthy too). Fat is not ugly (skinny people can be ugly too and ugly is subjective, anyway). Fat is not evil (skinny people can be evil too). Fat is not slow (skinny people can be slow too). Fat is not an insult. We shouldn't ban people from calling people ""fat"". We should remind people that fat is not an insult, it's an adjective.

And as you'll see in this kickass documentary trailer, fat can be f*cking phenomenal.

headline: Welp, Here's A Head-Shakingly Sexist Ad From The 60's
lede:
So, this ad was put out by Kelly Services, a temporary staffing agency, in the 1960s.

Let's, for a moment, bypass the creepy, Mad Men-esque vibes here and focus on some of the words. The basic proposition here is that temp workers are better than full-time staffers because a) they're cheaper and b) they're completely expendable.

This is a message that hasn't been lost on corporate America, who have been hiring temp workers at record rates since the 2008 recession. And this 50+ year-old premise hasn't changed either; it's way easier to exploit someone when they've got no leverage.

Okay, now we can go back to the creepy, sexist overtones here because wtf...

I have been reading up on a particular food source that is literally everywhere, and the more I read (and eat), the more I'm intrigued. We need a better way to get the billions of people on this planet the protein they need to survive, I think this woman has a pretty neat approach to solving this problem.

headline: We All Love To Get Our Packages In A Hurry. Here's The Dirty Secret Behind How They Do That.
lede:
Listen, I really don't want to be that guy. I, too, love receiving my packages promptly and am no stranger to the giddy sensation of seeing that cardboard box waiting for me at my doorstep.

But everything has its costs, and to make this modern luxury a reality, companies like Amazon, WalMart, and others are creating an entire sector of low-paid, under-protected temporary workers who are routinely boxing and shipping items they couldn't afford to buy. Here's an inside look at how it's changing the American economy and degrading working conditions for everyone.

headline: When People Die On The Job In ‘Accidents,’ What Really Happens?
lede:
There are days like Workers Memorial Day that are set aside in the United States to remember those who died on the job. But really, if we paid more attention to what we were doing and if companies really had to enforce their own safety standards rather than skimping on them, we could save people rather than remember them when they're gone.

Warning: while these are actors and computer-generated animation, the effect is the same as watching someone get seriously injured or killed.

headline: Why Is It So Hard To Figure Out Whether Or Not Our Shampoos Are Slowly Poisoning Us?
lede:
There's a lot of debate about whether the chemicals we use every day are just fine ... or are slowly poisoning us. I'm hoping it's the former, but until we are so sure we'd bet a baby elephant's life on it, I wish the FDA wouldn't let companies test them on us.

Ask yourself if you're using more or fewer products than the average man or woman (1:55), what Estee Lauder's motives are (4:15), and WTF is up with the FDA (from 5:25)?!

headline: Watch The Spread of Walmart Across The Country In One Horrifying GIF
lede:
When big box stores (I’ll leave it to you to decide just WHICH big-box stores) come to town, they almost always shut down all the mom-and-pop stores in the area they open in. And it’s a pretty simple formula: 1) Move in. 2) Open doors with much lower prices than anyone else. 3) Get employees on welfare and Medicaid, because you don’t want to pay well or provide medical insurance. 4) Force smaller shops out of business. 5) Raise prices, because now you’re the only game in town. 6) Rinse, repeat 15 miles down the road.

I’ve heard some say, “capitalism works this way, and great for the owners of _INSERT_BIG_BOX_STORE_HERE_ that they’re able to do so well because at least they create jobs.” To them I say, “At what price?""

Note: this map only goes to 2006; it’s much worse now, believe it or not.

lede: So, Chevron’s using this law called the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations (RICO) Act to try to stop people from speaking up about pollution in the Ecuadorian Amazon. But RICO was actually created so that people engaging in racketeering — like, you know, mob bosses — could be charged. If Chevron gets away with this, well, it could mean something all of us should worry about.

headline: What Life Might Feel Like For Kids Near The Fukushima Disaster Area
lede: It has been a few years since an earthquake and tsunami caused a radiation leak at the Fukushima nuclear plant, but there are continuing consequences. Though many places in the Fukushima prefecture are still evacuated, parents in nearby areas are worried for their children's safety, and require kids to wear face masks and to limit their outdoor play. Decontaminating homes is expensive and time-consuming ... but is it possible to decontaminate nature? This short, animated film gives us a peek into how it might feel to see nature dangerous, rather than a playground.

headline: A Little Girl Who Cannot Play Outside, And The Startling Reason Why
excerpt: Have we forgotten about what's been happening in Japan?
lede:
It has been a few years since an earthquake and tsunami caused a radiation leak at the Fukushima nuclear plant, but there are continuing consequences. Though many places in the Fukushima prefecture are still evacuated, parents in nearby areas are worried for their children's safety, and require kids to wear face masks and to limit their outdoor play.

Decontaminating homes is expensive and time-consuming ... but is it possible to decontaminate nature? This short, animated film gives us a peek into how it might feel to see nature as dangerous, rather than a playground.

lede:

Sometimes they cry, and sometimes they need feeding. Who knew. So Ashley did what any mum would so and as she was purchasing $150 worth of items from the store, she asked if she could use one of the empty dressing rooms to feed him. They refused and were all like this.

But lo and behold, here is Saturday Night Live to hilariously convey the sheer ?!?!?!? of this moment.

headline: WARNING: This Type Of Dancing Can Lead To Severe Empowerment And A Strong Connection With Others
lede:
Valentine's Day is usually reserved for chocolates and those (really gross) candy hearts that say things like, ""Call Me."" (Seriously though. Are they just straight up made of chalk?) However, the past couple of years have been a little different. Eve Ensler, who penned ""The Vagina Monologues,"" has started a movement to end violence against women. And when I say ""movement,"" I really mean movement! People all over the world are getting together to dance and demand justice for survivors of violence.

The moves are really easy, I swear. The song is called, ""Break the Chain,"" and the instructions are underneath the video. This video shows some of last year's gatherings, and they are spectacular.

Forget the cheesy greeting cards. Let's spread a message that really matters.

headline: A Man Was Looking For The Person Who Saved Him From Committing Suicide — And He Found Him.
lede:
Jonny Benjamin was diagnosed with schizoaffective disorder when he was 20 years old. He lost hope for a 'normal' life and tried to take his own life.

But thankfully, he was stopped by the kindness of a stranger.

Jonny — now a mental health campaigner — decided to track the man who stopped him, Mike, and launched a huge internet campaign to find him. In two weeks the campaign went viral and Jonny found him. See the moment take place below.

headline: PRETTY URGENT STUFF: How One Big Oil Company Is Trying To Make It A Crime For People To Speak Up
lede: So, Chevron’s using this law called the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations (RICO) Act to try to stop people from speaking up about pollution in the Ecuadorian Amazon. But RICO was actually created so that people engaging in racketeering — like, you know, mob bosses — could be charged. If Chevron gets away with this, well, it could mean something all of us should worry about.

headline: We've Been Hearing About A Disease For 30 Years, But You Might Not Have Heard This Story Yet
lede:
In the filmmaker's own words, this is ""a story of resilience, love, activism, and hope."" Almost 30 years ago, a group of people began working on the project that turned into the AIDS Memorial Quilt to bring light a stigmatized illness that was claiming tens of thousands of lives.

We've been hearing about HIV and AIDS for nearly three decades now and so much progress has been made with treatment, but before we get too comfortable, let's remember this: over 30 million people are infected worldwide and over 50,000 are diagnosed in the United States every single year.

I'd love to see this film that tells the story of the AIDS Memorial Quilt, along with the stories of many who live with HIV and AIDS, but they need our help to make it happen.

headline: In 28 Years White People Will Become The Minority In America. Everyone Breathe.
lede:
According to Census figures, Whites* are projected to become the minority race in America by 2042. As a conservative estimate, I'd say there are probably going to be 219,402,392,302 thinkpieces written about this oncoming phenomenon. Here's why you should take it all with a grain of salt.

*In the official Census count ""Lebanese, Arab, Moroccan"" are also counted as White. This proves that a) race is a totally made up thing, b) somebody at the Census Bureau is hitting the sauce during work hours, or c) both.

headline: Here Is The Only Known Video Footage Of Anne Frank
lede:
Anne Frank is remembered as the young girl who was sadly a Jewish victim in the Holocaust, and the girl who wrote the wartime diary The Diary of a Young Girl which documented her personal experiences of World War Two.

The memory of her is mostly cast in black and white photos and the deeply personal words from her diary, but I had never actually seen footage of her — until now. I came across this footage of Anne as she watched a wedding procession from her window of her Amsterdam home, which is thought to be the only footage of her to exist. It is quite strange to see her as a normal girl in such an everyday situation, when she was always remembered as a normal girl in a harrowing situation.

headline: OK, Smarty Pants. You Think Our Problems Are So Bad, But Can You Point To Where Things Are Better?
lede:
There’s a vast disconnect between what we are now and what this country used to be. I know that makes me sound like an old man – and perhaps I am – but it’s true. We used to be a country that had a social compact that if you worked hard your whole life, you could build a decent savings, buy a house, retire with a great pension and healthcare, your kids could go to school and then get a great job and you didn’t have to worry about them living with you forever for lack of decent jobs. Here’s Robert Reich with some hints about how that came to be.

Oh, and YOU KIDS GET OFF MY LAWN!

headline: Why Are A Bunch Of People In The Amazon Being Treated Like Mobsters?
excerpt: Are you a corp that’s trying to dodge accountability? Chevron’s got just the thing!
lede: So, Chevron’s using this law called the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations (RICO) Act to try to stop people from speaking up about pollution in the Ecuadorian Amazon. But RICO was actually created so that people engaging in racketeering — like, you know, mob bosses — could be charged. If Chevron gets away with this, well, it could mean something we all should worry about.

headline: Billie Holiday With One Of The Most Powerful And Emotional Performances Ever Captured On Video
excerpt: This song was first recorded in 1939, and it opened the eyes of a nation.
lede:
Though it was originally written by someone else as a protest song of sorts, this is one of those gems that a singer just takes and totally owns from the start. “Strange Fruit” was shocking to many people when it came out originally in 1939. Even Ms. Holiday herself was initially reluctant to sing it, fearing retribution. Though many people knew that lynchings of African Americans in the South were relatively common, there was much resistance to ending it, since it was a very effective means of social control and political intimidation by Southern whites.

Because the song was so powerful and poignant for Holiday, there were some rules when she performed it: she would close the evening with the song, the waiters would stop service when the song began, and the room would be in total darkness except for a spotlight on Billie Holiday’s face. And there would be no encore.

Her voice is strong and impressive in this clip, but just look at the incredible expressiveness in her eyes as she gets through the haunting lyrics.

Trigger warning: lyrics about racial violence.

headline: Here's Pussy Riot Calling For Human Rights In Russia. In Brooklyn. With Madonna (?)
lede:
Pussy Riot were famed as the punk band in Russia who were imprisoned after they spoke out, and protested against, their government for their brutal repression of freedom. So when two of the Pussy Riot members* Maria Alyokhina and Nadezhda Tolokonnikova were eventually released, they stood on stage at a charity concert in America and continued to do what got them sent to jail in the first place: speaking out against the human rights violations in Russia. ""We will not forgive and we will not forget what the regime is doing to our fellow citizens. We demand a Russia that is free,"" Tolokonnikova shouted to the crowd — such. bad. asses.

Oh, and they were introduced by Madonna — because of course?

headline: Sexualization Is Fun For Everyone In This Simply Irresistible Gender Swap Homage To An 80's Classic
lede:
Ingrid Michaelson’s music video for her song “Girls Chase Boys” is a delightfully gender remixed homage to Robert Palmer’s 1988 music video for “Simply Irresistible.”

What I love most about it is that her background dancers are people of all races and genders and the camera treats them all equally. It's sexual without being sexist, and the end result is something that feels pretty empowering and straight up fun.

headline: Could Solitary Confinement In The U.S. Be Worse Than A Prison In Iran?
lede:
In 2009 three Americans were hiking near the Iran-Iraq border when they were captured and imprisoned. They were accused of being spies and kept in an Iranian prison without a trial for two years. (One hiker was released after a little over a year.) In this video, one of the hikers, Shane Bauer, tours an American prison's solitary unit to see how it compares to where he stayed in isolation in Iran for four long months.

Warning: A NSFW moment happens at around 2:42.

headline: So Fresh And So Green: The Ace Concrete Jungle In Milan
lede:
What's that over there? Is that two large building blocks in the middle of Milan covered in a rich, green forest? Why yes, yes it is.

In the Porta Nuova district of Milan, Italy there is a pair of residential towers that (will) host more than 900 trees on 8,900 square metres of terraces as part of a 'rehabilitation' of the historic district. Or in other terms: lots and lots of trees in a tiny, little space to make the place look prettier. An ace, creative idea.

Oh and just a side thought about the video: how much does the man at 0:23 in look like Hugh Jackman?! IS HE HUGH JACKMAN'S LONG LOST TWIN BROTHER.

headline: How Much Greater Are Wall Street Bonuses Than The Earnings Of Every Minimum Wage Worker In The US?
lede:
New research challenges a weirdly popular belief that was expressed in a few words by Michael Douglas in the 1987 movie, ""Wall Street.""

Researchers at the Institute for Policy Studies stacked recent Wall Street bonuses ( just their bonuses) next to the total earnings of every full-time minimum wage worker in the United States. My takeaway, crazy as it may sound, is that we should start thinking a little more like Wall Street when it comes to economic policy.

lede:
The Colorado River has been dammed (you could say “damned”) so many times that it no longer runs all the way to the ocean. It’s like a beat up marathon runner who’s collapsed before the finish line.

The good news is it’s fixable. But not everyone agrees about how we should do that, like Will Ferrell and Robert Redford. Their debate is getting a little nasty — and a lot of funny.

headline: Disney Slimmed Down An Iconic Figure And Here’s Why They Shouldn’t Even Have Gone There
excerpt: If you look at it from this perspective, an iconic Disney villain is really more of a hero.
lede:
Ursula (the Sea Witch from ""The Little Mermaid"") has always been considered a villain, but after listening to this, I'm thinking she's kiiiiiiind of a hero. To more people than you'd think.

Sure, Ariel had her itty-bitty seashell bikini, but Ursula ""made backfat f*cking sexy."" And we need more of that kind of bravery in pop culture, if you ask me.

headline: Why The Money Spent To Become A Teacher Goes Far Beyond That College Diploma
lede:
Sitting in class as a student, one of the last things you think about is where all of the classroom supplies came from. The school fairy, maybe?

But realistically, at least some of them probably came out of the teacher’s own wallet. The perks of being a teacher (educating future generations!) might outweigh the cost of staplers and bulletin boards, but isn’t it still alarming to see the financial hoops many must jump through just to do their jobs?

headline: A Famous Rapper's Expletive-Laden Takedown Of Hip Hop Homophobia That Everyone Should Hear
lede:
Okay, so rapper T-Pain's vocabulary is ... well ... colorful. Don't play this out loud in an office setting unless you work for a bunch of sailors ... or put some headphones on.

T-Pain, despite the not-so-nice overuse of the B-word among others, delivers what, at the crux of this interview, is a well-intentioned, reasoned smackdown on his colleagues in the hip hop industry. It's pretty amazing hearing this come out of a leader in a music genre which has a proven track record of homophobia. Let T-Pain start a trend!

lede:

headline: These Folks Have Lived Through Some Dark Days. Here’s How They’re Changing That — With Garbage.
lede: Remember when the most powerful typhoon ever recorded smashed into the Philippines and created a living hell for millions of people? It’s easy to forget for those of us who don’t have to face the aftermath day after day. But it’s important to remember that those who were affected have been living through the darkest days of their lives. This amazing project is helping to change that by bringing a little light back to devastated communities like Tacloban.

headline: Why Being A Teacher Costs A Lot More Than The Price Of A Teaching Degree
lede:
Sitting in class as a student, one of the last things you think about is where all of the classroom supplies came from. The school fairy, maybe?

But realistically, at least some of them probably came out of the teacher’s own wallet. The perks of being a teacher (educating future generations!) might outweigh the cost of staplers and bulletin boards, but isn’t it still alarming to see the financial hoops many must jump through just to do their jobs?

headline: Afraid Of Robots? You Should Be. They're Real And Using You To Change Elections And Media Freedom.
excerpt: See it go down in real time.
lede:
Feeling a little meh about protecting your online life from hackers? Think you don't have much to hide? Turns out a weak password can accidentally make you a mercenary for malicious organizations and actions in real life.

That means, by not taking care with your online identity, you could be accidentally shutting down websites with strong independent voices, influencing political events or silencing opposing voices in media, around the world.

headline: This Is The Only Gap That Matters For Working Women
lede:
April 8 each year is recognized as the symbolic day that women finally catch up to the pay that men earned last year. See anything wrong with that picture?

And just to head off some comments like ""Well, since women have to take time off to take care of kids and therefore are not as career-focused as men, of course they will make less, blah blah blah."" I will answer in advance with this: Tellya what, I'd prefer to live in a society that truly values children and rewards those who take the time to nurture them by allowing mothers and fathers to take paid time off as needed, and not penalizing them for doing so.

And just to head off some comments like ""Well, since women have to take time off to take care of kids and therefore are not as career-focused as men, of course they will make less, blah blah blah."" I will answer in advance with this: Tellya what, I'd prefer to live in a society that truly values children and rewards those who take the time to nurture them by allowing mothers and fathers to take paid time off as needed, and not penalizing them for doing so.

headline: Close Your Eyes And Think Of The Last Ad You Saw. Did It Leave You Better Or Worse? How Do You Know?
lede: The average person spends over 7 hours a day interacting with media, and is exposed to over 5,000 marketing messages daily. Think of the last ad you saw. Was it bad for the world? Or good? And how can you tell? The founder of Beware of Images, Sergio ""Topo"" Toporek says, ""the only truly dangerous media, is the unexamined media"" and he is creating a film to help us all analyze it more critically.

lede:
Feeling a little meh about protecting your online life from hackers? Think you don't have much to hide? Turns out a weak password can accidentally make you a mercenary for malicious organizations and actions in real life.

That means, by not taking care with your online identity, you could be accidentally shutting down websites with strong independent voices, influencing political events or silencing opposing voices in media, around the world.

headline: The Secret Plan To Keep Some Parts Of The Population From Voting

This video was from the 2012 election, but it gives you a good idea of what happened in a lot of metropolitan areas. And the biggest clue why they're making it all worse for the next voting day? What the guy says at 02:30.

headline: A Musical Where The Only Person Successfully ""Acting Black"" Is An Asian Woman
lede:
I used to be in plays in high school. As all adolescent theater dorks do, between rehearsals and harmonising to Top 40 songs in the car, I considered going into acting. I ended up deciding against it mostly because I thought I was really terrible acting the way I saw most black actors acting in television and movies in the late '90s.

Still, minority actors are faced with that dilemma all the time: should they refuse to become a stereotype, or should they actually get to work?

lede: I carry myself like Frasier Crane or Tony Stark or anyone James Spader has ever played because I grew up in a family that had a lot of love, but didn't have a lot of money. So, I do what a lot of middle to lower class people do, which is dream of being rich. That doesn't end up happening for most of us, so we fight for what we have, even if the connection is a tenuous one. Take a look at what happened to Amanda Brown's family when they were confronted with this very dilemma.

headline: I’d Love To Hear One Good Reason Why Parents Like These Can't Adopt In Your State
lede:
We’re in the middle of the process of attempting to adopt children, and I’ve suspected all along that being straight, white, and middle-aged gives us a huge leg up. In fact, we have some gay friends that we met along the way who are also trying to adopt, and I always wonder how many kids they’re not considered for because of bias and bigotry inherent in the system.

Turns out, that bigotry can be codified into law.

headline: A Typhoon Hit The Philippines, But Few Have Told The Story Of The Aftermath — Until Now
lede:
Do you remember the typhoon that hit the Phillippines in November 2013? It struck the island, killing more than 6,000 people and leaving more than 4 million homeless... but the country has been left largely off the radar in global news since then. Why? Because the country isn't America?

This photo series below shows what one aid worker has seen since the typhoon hit, and how children are struggling to survive in a country heavily affected by the natural disaster.

headline: I Walked For Breast Cancer. I Got My Ribbon. Now Where’s My Cure?
lede: I was suspicious about this documentary when I first heard about it. My grandmother is a breast cancer survivor and public events always seemed like supportive opportunities to talk openly about the disease and its impact on women and families everywhere. However, I’m so glad I watched this. It’s important to question institutions and find out more information about where your money goes (and why) before investing in any cause.

headline: The NFL Makes Over 9 Billion Dollars Annually, Guess How Much Money It Pays In Taxes?
lede: I'm a big football fan. But It shocked me when I found out that the NFL, which has no trouble making money, pays nothing in taxes (nor does any professional sports league). And it hasn't for the past 40 years. This organization has no trouble turning a profit thanks to lucrative TV rights, but taxpayers are getting burned by this one little clause in a 40-year old bill. Thankfully some people are trying to change that.

headline: A Lot Can Go Wrong In Childbirth, But $1.53 Could Mean The Difference Between Life And Death
lede:
When you're pushing something the size of a small watermelon out of your body, there's a lot that can go wrong. Like, deadly wrong. That's the bad news. The good news is that having access to three affordable drugs can save a lot of lives.

Wouldn't you spend $1.53 if it meant keeping your mom alive? (I don't know your mom, so maybe not, but I'll give her the benefit of doubt for the purposes of making an excellent point. Deal with it.)

headline: There Are Some Things Women Should Not Have To Do In A Bathroom Stall
lede: Here's the thing. There are these things called breasts. They are body parts. They serve many functions. Sometimes, they feed babies. That last part is pretty important. When a woman is breastfeeding she is not trying to offend you with her breasts. She is giving life-sustaining food to her child. Do you really want her to have to do that behind closed doors -- especially if those closed doors are the doors of an unsanitary public bathroom stall?

headline: You Have Two Hours To Build A Chapel And Marry 24 Same-Sex Couples. Who You Gonna Call?
lede: When New York legalized same-sex marriage in 2011, a small army of volunteers and city employees got together to plan, prepare, and even pay for 24 weddings in Central Park -- in a single day. Watch the whole thing unfold in this video, from building ""chapels"" from the ground up to many, many happy couples finally saying I Do! I was one of those volunteers, and I still get the happy chills when I remember it. I hope you enjoy this as much as I loved being part of it!

headline: An Open Letter To The 11-Year-Old Suicidal Girl He Used To Be
lede: When Ethan was 11 years old, he struggled with understanding why he was unhappy. He knew something about his body wasn't quite as it should be, and his unhappiness manifested itself in suicidal thoughts and depression. As he got older, he came to realize he was struggling with the fact that his gender identity didn't match his gender and began the long transition to becoming a man. This is his story.

headline: Watching A Black Woman Smile In The Face Of Racism Is Pure Delight. Then She Goes In For The Hug…
excerpt: I love watching racists getting humiliated. But even better is watching them be human.
lede: Mo Asumang is a German TV host and filmmaker, and what she pulls off in this video is amazing. Not only does Mo speak with groups of people who claim to hate her (which she does with grace and compassion), but she actually gets a few of them to let down their guard and reveal that they might just have a heart. It's fascinating to witness and probably the one and only context in which you can root for a racist — for them to stop being racist!

Only one city has more people packed into an area that size.

headline: Their Job Is To Report The News, But Now They've Become The News ... Front Page
lede: You've seen it all over the news. Being a war journalist has become extremely dangerous. According to the film, only two journalists were killed in WWI, but in the past two decades, almost one journalist a week has been killed. They've been kidnapped, tortured and even beheaded. War journalists are risking their mental health and their lives so the rest of us can see what's really happening in the world.
